title: Peppermint creams
subtitle: Simple fondant mint sweets, no cooking required
excerpt: Icing sugar worked with egg white and peppermint extract into a smooth, firm fondant, then cut into rounds and left to dry. No thermometer, no stove — a reliable beginner confection with a clean, sharp mint finish.
sourceNotes: Peppermint creams are among the oldest documented British confections. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, lists peppermint drops and fondant-based mint sweets under the confectionery chapter. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), documents icing-sugar fondant work using egg white as the binder. The egg-white-and-icing-sugar base is the standard domestic method before cooked fondant became widely accessible.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Work the dough in two batches if scaling up — large quantities stiffen quickly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a cool, dry tin for up to 2 weeks. Do not refrigerate — condensation softens the surface.

glossaryTerms.definition: A confection made from icing sugar worked with a liquid binder (egg white, water, or cooked sugar syrup) into a smooth, pliable paste. Raw fondant (icing-sugar-and-egg-white) sets firm at room temperature; cooked fondant (pâte à foncer) is more stable and used in professional patisserie. Peppermint creams and chocolate fondant fillings use raw fondant.

content.text: — icing sugar worked with egg white and peppermint extract until smooth, then cut and left to dry. They need no cooking: the egg white provides just enough moisture to bring the sugar to a pliable paste, and the rounds firm overnight at room temperature. The result is clean mint flavour, a slight give when bitten, and a dry surface that takes a chocolate dip well.

Use peppermint extract, not peppermint essence or oil. Extract is oil-based and strong — start with less than you think you need and add by the drop. 1 tsp in 350 g of icing sugar gives a sharp, clean mint; more than that can taste medicinal.

Put the sifted {{icing-sugar}} into a bowl. Beat the {{egg-whites}} lightly with a fork until broken up but not foamy. Add 1 tsp of peppermint extract (or to taste). Make a well in the icing sugar and pour in the egg white mixture. Work together with a fork, then your hands, until a smooth, pliable paste forms. It should feel like firm, non-sticky play dough. If it crumbles, add egg white a few drops at a time; if it sticks to your hands, sift in more icing sugar.

Dust the work surface lightly with icing sugar. Roll the paste out to about 7 mm thick — no thinner, or the rounds snap rather than give. Cut out rounds with a 4 cm cutter, re-rolling the offcuts until all the paste is used. Lay the rounds on a sheet of baking paper. Leave uncovered at room temperature for at least 1 hour, or overnight, until the surface is dry to the touch and the rounds have set firm.

For dipped peppermint creams, melt 150 g of dark chocolate over a pan of barely simmering water, then dip each round halfway, place on baking paper, and leave to set at room temperature. Do not refrigerate — the chocolate will bloom.
